name: Cooper's Hawk
ecosystem: Forest edge, deciduous and mixed forest, suburban parks and neighborhoods with mature trees, riparian corridors.
conservation: Least Concern (IUCN). Stable or increasing. Suburban colonization contributed to population growth post-DDT. Trichomonosis contemporary concern.

vocalization.description: Rapid, loud series: cak-cak-cak (also kek-kek-kek). Faster, higher-pitched than Goshawk alarm.
vocalization.function: Nest defense, territorial alarm, suburban feeder hunting context
vocalization.notable: Suburban colonization means this alarm is now heard in Seattle residential neighborhoods. Familiar to urban birders.

size.id_challenge: Size overlap with Sharp-shinned Hawk. Rounded tail tip (Cooper's) vs. square/notched (Sharp-shin) is primary field mark.

ecology.habitat: Forest edge to suburban. Breeds in Seattle parks and residential areas with mature trees.
ecology.prey: Medium birds (robins, starlings, mourning doves, house sparrows). Ambush-and-short-pursuit. Low approach, rapid rise-and-stoop at feeders.
ecology.urban_adaptation: Successful suburban breeding colonization in past 40-50 years. Volunteer Park, Seward Park, U-District, Bellevue, Kirkland documented.
ecology.goshawk_partition: Goshawk = mature forest interior. Cooper's = forest edge, suburban. Complementary habitat use.

connections.description: Wellwater Conspiracy (E=6). Both occupy middle ground: medium-scale project, adaptable, suburban bridge.
Northern Goshawk. Largest accipiter. Cooper's is the medium of the trio.
Sharp-shinned Hawk. Smallest accipiter. The ID problem pair.
Bald Eagle -- also DDT-recovered. Cooper's Hawk shares the DDT decline / 1972 recovery arc.

milestones: MOST LIKELY ACCIPITER FOR SEATTLE RESIDENTS
